reasoning: Definite article before nouns in all displayed examples (e.g., 'the whole world', 'the conditions').
Genitive/partitive marker before noun phrases in all contexts such as 'value of a whole' and 'pity for ... existences'.
Mostly marks infinitival verbs ('to be thought', 'to be wanted'), with one nominal phrase use not dominant.
Occurs as copular/linking verb: 'pleasure alone is good', 'it is  a means'.
Used to introduce clauses in sampled contexts ('that is found', '...that Ethics can have...').
Pronominal argument/reference in examples like 'it is absurd' and 'such as what it commands'.
Spatial/abstract preposition before noun phrases ('in this case', 'in our present state').
Indefinite article before countable nouns across examples ('a marked distinction', 'a separate name').
Auxiliary/copular form in phrases like 'to be good' and 'to be done'.
Coordinating conjunction joining clauses and noun phrases in the contexts.
Relative/wh-pronoun-like in examples: 'the difficulty which is found', 'emotions which is necessary'.
First-person plural subject in discussion passages ('we leave', 'we shall have').
Technical ethical predicate often used as a substantive ('good is good', 'good in themselves').
Frequently introduces comparisons or frames ('as above stated', 'as regards').
Refers to prior proposition or term ('this must not be taken', 'denote this and only this').
Direct negation before predicates in all sampled contexts.
Third-person plural copular/auxiliary in constructions like 'they are not' and 'qualities are so'.
Interrogative/relative pronoun introducing embedded nominal content ('what is cognised', 'what exists').
Coordination of alternatives appears in explicit binary options throughout contexts.
Prepositional marker introducing agent/instrument causes (e.g., 'attributed by', 'as judged by').
Perfect/auxiliary behavior in 'has followed', 'have to answer'; not a lexical object noun use here.
First-person singular subject in personal argument ('I have', 'I contend').
Contrasts proposition branches in passages like '... but something else ...'.
Epistemic modal in contexts like 'may be', 'may involve', marking possibility.
Preposition meaning purpose/pursuant to in contexts such as 'care for knowledge for its own sake'.
Explicitly introduces conditionals ('if we consider', 'if this were').
Comitative/associative preposition before noun groups ('with the importance', 'with regard').
Third-person plural referential pronoun recurring as discussion subject ('they may', 'they had').
Sentence-level modifier limiting scope, as in 'it only states', 'only connection'.
Core noun of the treatise, appearing as 'consciousness of pleasure' and 'pleasure is ...'.
Abstract noun in phrases like 'value of' and 'degree of value'.
Indefinite article before singular nouns ('an action', 'an end').
Quantifier/determiner introducing unspecified members ('any fact', 'any objection').
Auxiliary in perfect constructions ('has given', 'has followed').
Ability/possibility modal in contexts such as 'can be', 'can be seen'.
Distributive determiner over noun sets ('all the actions', 'all those actions').
Numeral in constructions 'one of', 'one pleasure', 'one or two'.
Future/modal auxiliary in 'will be', 'will produce', 'will imply'.
Third-person masculine pronoun referring to agents discussed by name ('he holds', 'he maintained').
Degree modifier in 'so that', 'so much', and adverbial linking uses.
Attributive in 'other questions' and 'other actions', marking contrastive otherness.
Source/origin preposition in 'from this', 'from the facts'.
Negative determiner in 'no objection', 'no one', not part of wh- questions.
Modal auxiliary in hypothetical/counterfactual contexts ('would produce', 'would be').
Possessive determiner before nouns ('its additional good', 'its sole constituents').
Determining adjective in 'such goods', 'such propositions', introducing a noun class.
Deontic/modal auxiliary in 'must have', 'must be', 'must consider'.
Mostly used as verb 'it means' and 'the proposition means', not the noun 'means of'.
Concrete/abstract entity noun in 'this thing' and 'such things'.
Discussed repeatedly as an item of inquiry ('the question'), including direct titles.
Entity noun in 'whole has value', 'whole proposition' and related ethics arguments.
Plural count noun ('admirable things', 'things in themselves') across contexts.
Discipline name as noun in section headings and subject references.

Demonstrative determiner in 'these questions', 'these relations'.
